results.content:
Challenges of Quantum GP Bandit Optimization: Theorem 1 [srinivas2010]). ‣ 2.2 Quantum GP Bandit Optimization ‣ 2 Problem Setting ‣ Balancing Expressivity and Learnability in Quantum Kernel Bandit Optimization") shows that the cumulative regret scales with the maximum information gain γT\gamma\_{T}, a kernel-dependent quantity that measures how informative the noisy observations are about the unknown f∗f^{\}. In particular, GP models with large γT\gamma\_{T} are harder to learn and typically incur higher regret. [...] ### 2.2 Quantum GP Bandit Optimization

We approach the quantum kernelized bandit problem using quantum GP models. Leveraging the equivalence between RKHS and GPs, we encode the inductive bias f∗∈ℋκQf^{\}\in\mathcal{H}\_{\kappa\_{Q}} by placing a GP prior over f∗f^{\} with the quantum kernel, i.e., f∗∼GP​(0,κQ​(⋅,⋅))f^{\}\sim{\rm GP}(0,\kappa\_{Q}(\cdot,\cdot)). Assuming Gaussian noise ηt∼𝒩​(0,σ2)\eta\_{t}\sim\mathcal{N}(0,\sigma^{2}), the posterior over f∗​(𝐱)f^{\}(\mathbf{x}) is also a GP, with mean μT​(𝐱)\mu\_{T}(\mathbf{x}) and variance σT​(𝐱,𝐱)\sigma\_{T}(\mathbf{x},\mathbf{x}) given by standard formulas (see Appendix A). [...] We investigate Gaussian process (GP) bandit optimization with quantum kernels, assuming the mean reward function lies in the reproducing kernel Hilbert space (RKHS) induced by the quantum kernel. This setting is motivated by NISQ-era tasks such as quantum control, state preparation and variational quantum algorithms. While quantum kernels can offer a ‘quantum advantage’ via domain-specific inductive biases, naïvely using full, high-dimensional kernels increases model complexity and information gain, leading to higher cumulative regret and poor learnability. To address this, we propose projected quantum kernels and classical kernel approximation techniques that reduce feature dimensionality while preserving key quantum properties. Using these approximate kernels, we develop misspecified GP

results.title: Quantum Non-Linear Bandit Optimization
results.content: 1. Introduction Non-linear bandit optimization, a.k.a., Gaussian process bandits, kernelized bandits, or Bayesian optimization, is a sequential decision making-based machine learning task that aims at solving a black-box optimization problem. Due to its black-box nature, it has been successfully applied in many important real-world applications where objective functions are difficult to define explicitly, e.g., hyperparameter tuning [37, 51], neural architecture search [26, 49], drug discovery [28, 41], and materials science [19, 57]. Non-linear bandit optimization also enjoys solid theoretical guarantees. In literature, researchers usually define simple regret and cumulative regret to capture the convergence behavior of a certain algorithm, and many regret upper bounds have been [...] QUANTUM NON-LINEAR BANDIT OPTIMIZATION of quantum computing. Later Q-GP-UCB  and QMCKernelUCB  studied the quantum Bayesian optimization, generalizing  to non-convex and non-linear settings, still achieving the O(poly log T) regret bound. However, [16, 24] both heavily rely on the reproducing kernel Hilbert space assumption, which suffers from the curse of dimensionality. The problem is that, in practice, many input data sit in high-dimensional spaces. Again in drug discovery, for example, the dimension of protein sequences usually ranges from thousands  to even millions . Then either O(d 3 2 x (log T) 3 2 ) (linear kernel) or O(T 3dx 2v+dx ) (Mat´ ern kernel with v being a kernel parameter) regret bound  become vacuous when input dimension dx goes to millions, which further prevents [...] OPT2025: 17th Annual Workshop on Optimization for Machine Learning Quantum Non-Linear Bandit Optimization Zakaria Shams Siam ZSIAM@ALBANY.EDU University at Albany, State University of New York Chaowen Guan GUANCE@UCMAIL.UC.EDU University of Cincinnati Chong Liu CLIU24@ALBANY.EDU University at Albany, State University of New York Abstract We study non-linear bandit optimization where the learner maximizes a black-box function with zeroth order function oracle, which has been successfully applied in many critical applications such as drug discovery and materials design. Existing works have showed that with the aid of quantum computing, it is possible to break the classical Ω( √ T) regret lower bound and achieve the new O(poly log T) upper bound. However, they usually assume that the

Extended version — 

## Introduction

Non-linear bandit optimization, a.k.a., Gaussian process bandits, kernelized bandits, or Bayesian optimization, is a sequential decision making-based machine learning task that aims at solving a black-box optimization problem. Due to its black-box nature, it has been successfully applied in many important real-world applications where objective functions are difficult to define explicitly, e.g., hyperparameter tuning (wu2020practical), neural architecture search (kandasamy2018neural), drug discovery (korovina2020chembo), and materials science (frazier2016bayesian). [...] In addition to its successful real-world applications, non-linear bandit optimization also enjoys solid theoretical guarantees. In literature, researchers usually define simple regret and cumulative regret to capture the convergence behavior of a certain algorithm, and many regret upper bounds have been established under different assumptions and with different kernels. All these positive theoretical results further contribute to more applications of non-linear bandit optimization. However, unfortunately an Ω​(T)\Omega(\sqrt{T}) cumulative regret lower bound (scarlett2017lower) cannot be further improved. What does that mean? It implies that given total TT rounds, no algorithm can incur cumulative regret less than Ω​(T)\Omega(\sqrt{T}) asymptotically.
